Deep Mind's Alphafold database released predictions for more than 200 million protein structures. That is not just a research milestone. That is centuries worth of experimental biology compressed into a public digital resource. More than 3 million researchers across over 190 countries now use it. MIT's Boltzgen model was tested across 26 targets in eight separate wet laboratories, demonstrating that AI generated proteins can actually be fabricated and studied in the real world, not just simulated, built, measured, verified. Let that sink in. AI can now design brand new proteins that have never existed in nature at atomic precision. Proteins are not abstract concepts. They are the machinery of life. One gram of DNA can theoretically store about 215 pabytes of data. The world's 175 zetabytes of digital information could fit into just 81 kg of DNA. Another estimate suggests that 1.8 zetabytes of global data could fit into just 4 g. 4 g smaller than a teaspoon. Biology does not merely resemble computation. In terms of storage density, it surpasses anything we've built in silicone. Once you realize DNA is programmable code stored in ultra dense biological hardware, the next question becomes unavoidable. What happens when the cost of reading that code collapses? Because that is exactly what happened next. The genome cost collapse. The first human genome cost roughly $2.7 to3 billion to sequence. It took years of coordinated international effort. By 2014, that cost had dropped to around $1,000 per genome. By 2024, sequencing could be as low as 600 bucks. In 2025, some whole genome tests cost around $400. And companies in China have announced plans to push that number down to roughly $100 per person. From $3 billion to potentially a hundred bucks in just a couple of decades. That is not gradual improvement. That is a collapse in cost. And whenever cost collapse, data explodes. We've seen this pattern before. When storage became cheap, the internet exploded. When compute became cheap, AI models exploded. Now genome sequencing is collapsing in cost, which means biological data is about to expand at scale. Once that data begins flowing into AI systems, we move beyond analyzing text, images, and speech. AI begins analyzing life itself. And that is where the next transformation begins. Because when you combine massive biological data sets with machine learning models trained on them, drug discovery stops looking like chemistry and starts looking like engineering. AI turns drug discovery into engineering. For decades, drug discovery looked like organized guessing. Scientists would screen millions of molecules, most of them failing. A few would survive, years would pass. It was slow, expensive, and brutally inefficient. Now, something different is happening. Venture capital funding for AI oriented biotech and healthcare startups peaked at around $ 122.5 billion in 2021. It dipped during the broader tech slowdown, then recovered to $6.7 billion in 2024.
